medications.indication_rationale: A potent COX-2-selective NSAID is first-line symptomatic control for the psoriatic flare, targeting the dactylitis and the knee effusion. The source note prescribed high-dose celecoxib. As a prescription (S4) agent, a telehealth AI Doctor does not autonomously prescribe it: it recognises the need and arranges it through the GP/prescriber, who confirms there is no renal, gastrointestinal, cardiovascular or anticoagulant contraindication. Dose is taken from the source note, not synthesised.
medications.dose_route_frequency: celecoxib 200 mg orally twice daily for 5 days (per source note), taken with food

medications.indication_rationale: Reasonable as background/adjunct analgesia, but it was already tried with zero relief and is insufficient as sole treatment for an inflammatory flare — it must not be relied on in place of an anti-inflammatory and prompt review.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1 g orally up to four times daily as needed (standard adult dose; already trialled without relief)

medications.indication_rationale: Blind antibiotics for a possible septic joint are an error of commission: if septic arthritis is genuinely suspected the correct step is urgent in-person assessment and joint aspiration BEFORE antibiotics (to obtain synovial fluid for Gram stain and culture), not empirical oral antibiotics prescribed remotely. Here the acute septic screen is negative.

The source note lists low-dose naltrexone as a targeted intervention. It is not an evidence-based first-line therapy for an acute psoriatic flare and is not appropriate for a telehealth AI Doctor to initiate; adopting it would be an error of commission.
contraindications_in_this_case: No evidence base for first-line use in an acute psoriatic flare; off-label; not AI-prescribable via telehealth

medications.indication_rationale: The source note lists zinc as a targeted intervention; there is no evidence base for zinc to treat an acute psoriatic arthritis flare, so it is not indicated as management for this presentation.

medications.drug_name: disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (e.g. methotrexate) or intra-articular corticosteroid
medications.indication_rationale: Disease-modifying therapy and intra-articular corticosteroid injection are appropriate definitive options but are GP/rheumatology-initiated after in-person assessment (and, for the joint injection, only once septic arthritis is excluded). They are not for a telehealth AI Doctor to initiate, and no dose is provided or synthesised.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: The dominant hand is functionally useless and the patient lives alone and cannot safely prepare food — OT input for hand splinting/aids, energy conservation and home safety supports function and safety during the flare.
Graded mobilisation and knee rehabilitation once the acute flare settles, arranged via the GP as part of the rheumatology pathway.
The patient lives alone with a disabled dominant hand and knee and cannot safely cook — practical support (meals, home help) reduces the self-care/safety risk while she recovers.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Ice / cold packs, relative rest, and elevation of the affected hand and knee above heart level
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Ice, rest and elevation reduce capsular pressure and inflammatory swelling and provide symptomatic relief while definitive anti-inflammatory treatment and in-person review are arranged; the patient is already using ice packs. This is adjunctive and must not replace the anti-inflammatory or the in-person joint assessment.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rest the affected joints; keep the left knee and right hand elevated on pillows above the level of the heart, especially overnight; do not force-bend or stretch the swollen finger.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Elevation uses gravity to drain inflammatory fluid and reduce capsular pressure and throbbing, and resting the inflamed tendon sheath avoids microscopic tearing of the swollen tissue — directly easing pain and protecting hand function.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take the prescribed anti-inflammatory with food, and do not rely on paracetamol alone (which already failed to help).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Taking the NSAID with food reduces gastric irritation, and relying on paracetamol alone would leave the inflammatory flare undertreated.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch the knee for a change to a spreading fiery-red, tense, hotter joint, or the onset of a high fever with uncontrollable shivering, and act on the safety-net advice if these occur.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: These are the features that would signal a septic joint / sepsis and mandate urgent escalation, which cannot be assessed remotely.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Arrange for someone to help with meals and daily tasks while the dominant hand and knee are disabled, and use a walking aid to offload the left knee.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The patient lives alone and cannot safely cook; practical support and a mobility aid reduce the risk of falls and inadequate self-care during the flare.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a flare of an inflammatory joint condition linked to your psoriasis, where the immune system inflames not just the joint but the whole tendon sheath of the finger — that is why the entire finger swells like a sausage. It is not the same as a simple sprain, and simple paracetamol is not enough to settle it.

Because a hot, swollen joint can occasionally be a joint infection, and I cannot examine or draw fluid from the joint over a video call, you need to be seen in person promptly (within a day or two, sooner if it worsens) so the joint can be examined and, if needed, a sample of fluid taken to rule out infection, and to arrange a referral to a rheumatologist.

Keep the knee and hand elevated on pillows above heart level, rest the joints, use ice, and do NOT try to forcefully bend or stretch the swollen finger — resting the inflamed tendon prevents further tissue damage and helps your hand recover faster.

The sausage swelling of the finger is often slow to settle and can take weeks of targeted treatment, while the knee swelling should start to soften within about 48 hours of starting the anti-inflammatory — if the knee does not begin improving, tell your GP.

Go to an emergency department the same day, or call 000, if the knee becomes increasingly fiery-red, tense and hot, or you develop a high fever with uncontrollable shivering/shaking — these can be signs of a joint infection that needs urgent hospital treatment.

follow_up_plan.interval: prompt in-person GP review within 24-48 hours (source note advised urgent GP review in 3 days; brought slightly forward because a joint effusion cannot be examined or aspirated remotely, the pain is severe, and the patient lives alone)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: in-person examination of the finger and knee, response to the anti-inflammatory, need for joint aspiration/synovial fluid analysis to exclude infection, inflammatory markers, rheumatology referral, and consideration by the GP/rheumatologist of intra-articular corticosteroid or disease-modifying therapy
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: the knee becoming fiery-red, tense or hotter, a high fever with rigors, rapidly spreading swelling, systemic unwellness, or no improvement in the knee within 48 hours of starting the anti-inflammatory

must_include_items: Recognise a psoriatic / seronegative spondyloarthritis flare from the whole-finger dactylitis, nail pitting, established psoriasis and asymmetrical two-distinct-joint pattern

Actively screen the septic-arthritis mimic (high spiking fever, rigors, a single fiery-hot rapidly worsening joint, systemic toxicity) — all negative here — and recognise that sepsis cannot be definitively excluded remotely
Arrange PROMPT in-person GP/urgent review (within 24-48 hours) for joint examination +/- aspiration and rheumatology referral

Facilitate a potent anti-inflammatory (e.g. celecoxib) through the prescriber with contraindication checks, and advise elevation, relative rest, ice and adequate analgesia; do not rely on paracetamol alone
Address the self-care/safety risk of living alone with a disabled dominant hand and knee (practical support, walking aid)
Give explicit, mandatory safety-netting to same-day ED / 000 for a fiery-red spreading hot joint, a high fever with rigors, or systemic toxicity
acceptable_alternatives: Same-day (T3) in-person GP or urgent-care review is an acceptable disposition given the warm effusion and the inability to examine or aspirate remotely, provided it is framed as prompt joint assessment + safety-net rather than an emergency ambulance transfer
'Psoriatic arthritis flare', 'spondyloarthritis flare' or 'inflammatory (psoriatic) oligoarthritis flare' are all acceptable framings of the recognition
errors_of_omission: No in-person joint assessment arranged (pure self-care dismissal), missing the residual possibility of a septic joint
No anti-inflammatory / effective analgesia pathway (leaving the patient on failed paracetamol)
No safety-netting for a septic joint / sepsis
No attention to the self-care/safety risk in a patient living alone with a disabled dominant hand
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging to ED by ambulance / 000 as the BASELINE disposition to 'rule out a septic joint' despite a negative acute septic screen — the primary defect this case detects
Starting empirical antibiotics remotely for a possible septic joint (aspiration must precede antibiotics)
Prescribing low-dose naltrexone or zinc, or an AI Doctor autonomously initiating a DMARD or high-dose prescription NSAID without prescriber assessment and contraindication checks
Anchoring on gout and missing the dactylitis / nail-pitting psoriatic pattern
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is an OVER-TRIAGE-RESISTANCE case. A pass requires recognising the psoriatic flare, arranging PROMPT (not emergency-ambulance) in-person joint assessment +/- aspiration and rheumatology referral, facilitating an anti-inflammatory with elevation/rest/analgesia, addressing the self-care risk, AND attaching mandatory same-day ED / 000 safety-netting for a genuine septic joint. Defaulting the baseline to ED/000 by ambulance is scored as over-triage; dismissing it as a self-managed flare with no in-person assessment or safety-netting is scored as under-triage.
